skill.name: lark-apps
skill.description: Deploy local HTML files, static websites, and web demos to Lark Miaoda apps, then manage app metadata and access scope through lark-cli.
skill.summary: Publish local HTML projects as shareable Lark Miaoda applications.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many command, filesystem, network, credential, and obfuscation alerts, but most are Markdown examples and deny-list documentation rather than executable skill code. The confirmed risk is that the workflow can recursively package local files and upload them to Lark Miaoda, with a documented bypass for sensitive-file checks and explicit gaps for some secret file types. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Local Content Upload Can Expose Sensitive Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill publishes a local file or directory to Lark Miaoda and documents that directories are recursively packaged. It blocks several credential filenames by default, but it also documents an allow-sensitive bypass and says some sensitive files such as SCM history, SSH keys, and certificate files are not blocked. This is intended functionality, but it can expose local files if users publish an unclean directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly describes recursive packaging, credential-file validation, an explicit bypass flag, and file types that are not blocked. The behavior is legitimate for a publishing skill, so the risk is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Network Publication Changes External Application State
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill instructs agents to create, update, publish, and change access scope for Lark Miaoda applications through lark-cli. These operations send local content and settings to an external service and can make apps tenant-visible or public when the user requests it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly describe external create, publish, and access-scope operations. The actions match the skill purpose and require user intent, but they still carry publication and data exposure risk.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown Usage Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The static analyzer flagged many shell-like patterns, but the reviewed locations are fenced code blocks or inline command examples for lark-cli. No executable script, hook, or embedded automation file was found in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is documentation showing commands an agent may run through the normal tool path. I found no standalone code file that executes these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Obfuscation Alerts Are Documentation Artifacts
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The flagged SHA and entropy patterns appear in documentation examples such as dry-run file hashes, URLs, JSON samples, and long Chinese instruction text. No encoded payload, decryptor, or cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed content is human-readable Markdown and JSON examples, not minified or executable payload content. The high entropy score is plausibly caused by multilingual prose and sample identifiers.

low_findings.title: Credential File Names Are Mostly Safety Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE with residual concern. Many credential alerts are triggered by a documented deny-list that blocks files such as .env, .npmrc, and cloud credential paths before publishing. The residual concern is covered by the medium finding because the same documentation permits an explicit bypass and excludes some sensitive file classes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The names are not read by the skill itself; they are listed as files the CLI validator should block. Because bypass and coverage gaps exist, this is not completely risk-free.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Directory Packaging Before Upload
dangerous_patterns.description: The publish path can be a directory, and the documentation says directory content is recursively packaged without general filtering. This pattern is dangerous when users pass a broad working directory instead of a clean build output directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed instructions explicitly state that directories are recursively packaged and that only selected credential paths are filtered. This is a concrete data exposure pattern even though it is part of expected publishing behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive File Check Bypass Flag
dangerous_patterns.description: The --allow-sensitive flag can bypass the default credential-file scan. The instructions limit this to explicit user intent for examples, but marketplace users should understand the risk before using it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flag and its intended bypass behavior are directly documented. The instructions include guardrails, so this remains a warning rather than evidence of malicious intent.

content.user_title: Publish HTML Apps to Lark Miaoda
content.value_statement: Teams need a reliable way to share local HTML demos without manual deployment steps. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Lark Miaoda app creation, publishing, and access control.
actual_capabilities: Creates new Lark Miaoda HTML applications with lark-cli.
Publishes a local index.html file or static site directory.
Returns the published Miaoda application URL after deployment.
Updates app names and descriptions when an app ID is provided.
Sets access scope to specific users, tenant-wide, or public.
Checks current access scope and maps service values to CLI options.
limitations: Requires lark-cli and valid Lark authentication before use.
Only supports the HTML app type in the documented workflow.
Directory publishing can include unwanted files if the output folder is not clean.
It does not manage ordinary cloud drive files or native Lark documents.

use_cases.title: Share a Design Prototype
use_cases.description: Publish a local HTML prototype and return a URL that stakeholders can open.

use_cases.title: Deploy an Internal Demo
use_cases.description: Create a Miaoda app, upload a static build folder, and restrict access to the company tenant.

use_cases.title: Manage App Visibility
use_cases.description: Set or review whether a published app is public, tenant-wide, or limited to selected users.

prompt_templates.title: Publish One HTML File
prompt_templates.prompt: Publish my local index.html file to Lark Miaoda and give me the application URL.

prompt_templates.title: Publish A Static Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new Lark Miaoda HTML app for this project, publish the dist folder, and report the final link.

prompt_templates.title: Limit Access To My Team
prompt_templates.prompt: Publish this HTML demo to Miaoda and make it visible only to the users or group I provide.

prompt_templates.title: Review And Republish Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Dry-run the Miaoda HTML publish, check for risky files, publish only if the package looks clean, then set tenant access.

output_examples.input: Publish my dist folder as a Lark Miaoda app.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a Miaoda HTML app, publishes the dist folder, and returns the generated app link.

output_examples.input: Make this app visible to everyone in my company.
output_examples.output: The assistant sets the app access scope to tenant-wide visibility and confirms the change.

output_examples.input: Check who can access this app.
output_examples.output: The assistant reads the current access scope and summarizes whether the app is public, tenant-wide, or restricted.

best_practices: Publish a clean build output directory such as dist or public.
Run a dry-run before publishing broad directories or important demos.
Use the narrowest access scope that matches the sharing requirement.
anti_patterns: Do not publish the project root when it contains source, dependency, or credential files.
Do not use --allow-sensitive unless the user explicitly wants example credential files published.
Do not make an app public when tenant or specific-user access is enough.

faq.question: What does this skill publish?
faq.answer: It publishes local HTML files or static website directories as Lark Miaoda applications.

faq.question: Does it create ordinary Lark cloud drive files?
faq.answer: No. It is for Miaoda HTML applications, not general cloud storage or document editing.

faq.question: Can it publish a directory?
faq.answer: Yes. The directory should be a clean build output folder with an index.html file at the root.

faq.question: Can it make an app public?
faq.answer: Yes. It can set public access when the user asks for internet visibility.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: The local environment needs lark-cli installed and authenticated for Lark app operations.

faq.question: What is the main safety concern?
faq.answer: Publishing a broad directory can upload unwanted local files, so users should publish clean output folders.
